title: Veep

jokes.text: Did we win? / Oh, yeah. I'm the president, and you're the First Lady. / Oh, we would crush it.
jokes.setup: The vote totals are being announced and no one has won — a deadlocked convention.
jokes.punchline: Selina and Gary immediately slip into delusional fantasy about being president and First Lady 'crushing it,' completely ignoring the reality of no majority.
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy — Gary's role as Selina's delusional enabler and the fantasy of him as First Lady is deeply specific to their co-dependent relationship. The gag lands because both characters are completely earnest about something absurd.

jokes.text: You know, it's worse having to hear it from Mike.
jokes.setup: Mike, the former press secretary turned CBS News anchor, is calling the convention results on TV.
jokes.punchline: Selina's complaint isn't about losing — it's about the specific indignity of Mike being the one to announce it.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical. Tells you everything about Selina's ego and her contempt for Mike in six words. The specificity of 'Mike' as the wound makes this land hard. Her priorities are completely inverted — the loss is secondary to the humiliation of the messenger.

jokes.text: Guys, who gives a shit where people shit? / That is literally the point, Mother.
jokes.setup: Catherine is making a principled argument about North Carolina's bathroom bill being a disgrace.
jokes.punchline: Selina reduces the transgender bathroom rights issue to a scatological pun, accidentally making Catherine's point, then tries to claim agreement.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely clever wordplay that works on two levels — Selina's dismissive tone accidentally makes the literal argument for trans bathroom rights, and Catherine's perfect comeback ('That is LITERALLY the point') is a great straight-man response. Quotable and structurally elegant.

jokes.text: I'll whip the whips, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina says they need to get in touch with all the floor whips to ensure delegates hold.
jokes.punchline: Gary volunteers with complete earnestness to 'whip the whips' — a phrase that sounds absurd and is also functionally meaningless given his role.
jokes.explanation: Quick Gary character beat — his eagerness to help in ways he's unqualified to help is a running engine for his comedy. The doubled word is a minor verbal amusement.

jokes.text: Yeah, most importantly, he has a penis. Right, Governor?
jokes.setup: Selina is listing DeVito's political credentials — wounded warrior, Roman Catholic, two-time Ohio governor, 18 Electoral College votes.
jokes.punchline: She caps the list of credentials with 'he has a penis' and then addresses the disabled governor directly for confirmation, turning it into a cringe beat.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep escalation — the list of respectable credentials explodes into Selina's naked cynicism about electability, then the cringe deepens when she turns to ask the disabled war hero to confirm his own genitalia.

jokes.text: Might want to double-check that penis. / That's Kent's job.
jokes.setup: DeVito has just enthusiastically confirmed he's 'still with' Selina.
jokes.punchline: Someone quips about double-checking the penis, and Selina immediately delegates that to Kent with complete authority.
jokes.explanation: Two-beat joke with perfect economy. The setup is already dark (questioning DeVito's disability/gender), the punchline assigns the task to Kent in Selina's characteristic deadpan management style — treating the bizarre as routine bureaucracy.

jokes.text: Oh, no, Beautiful Mindfuck. You are not gonna desecrate my convention with more of this Muslim math nonsense.
jokes.setup: Jonah's campaign manager is challenging the official delegate count.
jokes.punchline: Furlong's nickname for Jonah ('Beautiful Mindfuck') and the phrase 'Muslim math nonsense' — a reference to Jonah's anti-math campaign platform.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. 'Beautiful Mindfuck' as a Furlong nickname for Jonah is inspired — it captures both Jonah's accidental impressiveness and the cognitive dissonance he produces. 'Muslim math nonsense' as a pejorative for basic arithmetic, delivered by a party leader as a matter-of-fact procedural objection, is exceptional satirical writing. Furlong's voice is completely distinct.

jokes.text: Because you were born with three umbilical cords wrapped around your neck, Hep C Kevin McHale.
jokes.setup: Jonah's surrogate is insisting their delegate math is correct using 'Christian math.'
jokes.punchline: Furlong offers a medical insult ('three umbilical cords wrapped around your neck') as an explanation for poor arithmetic, then deploys a bizarre celebrity comparison nickname.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insult vocabulary is a show highlight — the specificity of 'three umbilical cords' as a pseudo-medical explanation for stupidity is wildly creative. The celebrity comparison insult is a Furlong signature move, and 'Hep C Kevin McHale' is darkly absurd.

jokes.text: Oh, well, there it is, that casual Meyer attitude towards criminal enterprise, just like her husband. / Ex-husband. / Step-husband.
jokes.setup: Kemi accuses Selina of stealing South Carolina.
jokes.punchline: Kemi broadens the attack to include Selina's husband as a criminal, then the escalating corrections ('ex-husband'/'step-husband') reveal the complicated, equally damning marital history.
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the rapid-fire corrections that each make the situation worse — 'ex-husband' and 'step-husband' both carry distinct implications of dysfunction, and the fact that nobody can agree on the relationship status is funnier than the original insult.

jokes.text: If you can't figure out how to steal South Carolina, you have no business being president.
jokes.setup: Kemi is accusing Selina of stealing South Carolina as a crime.
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes electoral fraud as a basic competency requirement for the presidency.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. The complete moral inversion — treating electoral theft as a presidential job requirement — is perfectly calibrated Veep cynicism. It's also a perfect Selina line: contemptuous, self-serving, and delivered with total sincerity. High quotability, great structure.

jokes.text: Right now, every Sunday pundit and poli-sci major is treating this brokered convention like it's a big-titted college gymnast whose daddy fucked with her just enough that she'll do some dirty shit, but she can still cum.
jokes.setup: Furlong is explaining the political stakes of the brokered convention to the assembled candidates.
jokes.punchline: The extended sexual metaphor, escalating in specificity and depravity, is the entire joke — Furlong turning political analysis into a grossly inappropriate analogy.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's extended metaphors are a show staple and this one is characteristically depraved. It loses points for economy — the metaphor runs a bit long — but the character integration is strong. The joke that follows ('Hittin' a little close to home there, Roger') is actually the better payoff.

jokes.text: Hittin' a little close to home there, Roger, minus the big-titted part. / We've never had a complaint, have we?
jokes.setup: Furlong's gymnast metaphor has just ended.
jokes.punchline: Selina makes a self-deprecating reference (big-titted), then Furlong's comeback ('We've never had a complaint') is an implied sexual boast about Selina.
jokes.explanation: Quick two-beat exchange that deepens Furlong's depravity while Selina's self-aware self-deprecation makes her momentarily sympathetic. Furlong's response is perfectly timed and economical.

jokes.text: ...if we don't pillow-smother this sad grandma of a convention lickity-fuckin'-split, we are gonna wind up with four more years of Montez clogging up the White House plumbing with tampons.
jokes.setup: Furlong is pushing urgency on resolving the brokered convention.
jokes.punchline: The tampon-plumbing metaphor for a female president is characteristically Furlong — misogynistic but delivered as a matter-of-fact logistical concern.
jokes.explanation: A Furlong signature — the misogyny is the character, not the joke, but the specific image ('clogging up the White House plumbing') has visual absurdism that earns a modest laugh. Less sharp than his best lines.

jokes.text: And may the hairiest man zip-tie me to a slaughterhouse drainage grate and make tender hate to my rear and mouth, in that order, at his leisure.
jokes.setup: Will is asked what else needs to happen, and says 'may the best man win.' Furlong challenges him.
jokes.punchline: Will launches into an elaborately specific sexual masochism fantasy as the counterpart to 'may the best man win,' delivered with complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: Will's first line ('may the best man win') is admirably conventional and he's praised for it, then the 'what else?' prompt triggers an escalation into something astonishingly depraved. 'In that order, at his leisure' is the crucial detail that pushes this from shocking to genuinely funny. 'Beautiful. Well done, pig. Dismissed.' as Furlong's response is the perfect button. Near-perfect absurdist escalation.

jokes.text: Beautiful. Well done, pig. Dismissed.
jokes.setup: Furlong has just delivered his elaborate sexual fantasy.
jokes.punchline: Selina responds as if grading a performance — 'beautiful' + 'well done' + 'pig' + 'dismissed' — the manager-to-employee format applied to the most inappropriate speech imaginable.
jokes.explanation: Four words that function as a perfect button. The switch from praise ('beautiful,' 'well done') to dehumanization ('pig') to dismissal is a complete character arc in one breath. Extremely quotable. Exemplifies Selina's particular brand of contemptuous authority.

jokes.text: I've got the diving bell and the governor.
jokes.setup: Roger is urging Selina to put Kemi on the ticket as Veep, saying the combination will 'make everyone rock hard.'
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Kemi by gesturing toward DeVito (a disabled veteran who uses a wheelchair — the 'diving bell') as her ticket.
jokes.explanation: Elegant and dark. 'The diving bell' as a nickname for DeVito's wheelchair situation is both cruel and precise — a reference to 'The Diving Bell and the Butterfly' that characterizes both the man's physical state and Selina's callousness in a single phrase. Economy score is outstanding.

jokes.text: Shit, Will left. It was a good one.
jokes.setup: Furlong tells Selina to make a deal or something bad will happen, and asks 'Or what's gonna happen, Will?' — but Will has exited.
jokes.punchline: Furlong's acknowledgment that his own threat fell apart because his straight-man left, and his self-satisfied assessment that it 'was a good one' anyway.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke — Furlong uses Will as a comedy prop (his threats gain force from Will's silent menace), and the joke is that Furlong himself recognizes and laments the comedic machinery breaking down. 'It was a good one' is self-aware and perfectly Furlong. One of the episode's cleverest structural gags.

jokes.text: There is no place for Jonah Ryan in my administration. Or anywhere in the universe where the building blocks of life are present.
jokes.setup: Amy is trying to pitch Selina on Jonah; Selina cuts her off mid-sentence.
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissal escalates from political objection to cosmic banishment — Jonah is incompatible with life itself.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep escalation in two sentences. The first clause is normal political thinking, the second expands to the cosmological. 'Where the building blocks of life are present' is a beautifully specific scientific phrasing for an essentially emotional hatred. Selina-specific in its dramatic self-seriousness.

jokes.text: Your offer is as appealing as a Sriracha enema. So, fuck your offer, and fuck you. / It's always good to see ya, Amy. / You, too. I miss you guys.
jokes.setup: Amy is rejecting Selina's offer to the Jonah camp on behalf of her team.
jokes.punchline: The 'Sriracha enema' insult is followed by 'fuck you,' then an immediate warm sign-off ('I miss you guys') — Amy's professional ferocity coexisting with genuine affection.
jokes.explanation: The beat works on two levels: 'Sriracha enema' is a quality insult with good specificity, but the real punch is the tonal whiplash from 'fuck you' to 'I miss you guys' — which tells you everything about political Washington relationships.

jokes.text: You just broke North Carolina's transgender bathroom law. / What? / I've been fielding media requests all morning, Richard.
jokes.setup: Richard congratulates Selina on her keynote speech being a hit.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Selina accidentally became a transgender rights activist by using the wrong bathroom — and didn't know until now.
jokes.explanation: Good setup for a running gag that will pay off with Buddy Calhoun. Selina accidentally becoming a bathroom warrior is perfect — her most politically meaningful act in the episode is a complete accident.

jokes.text: New York Times? Krugman is positively creaming his khakis.
jokes.setup: Richard is excitedly reporting media praise for Selina's accidental trans-rights moment.
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'creaming his khakis' attributed to Paul Krugman, delivered by Richard with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: The specific choice of 'Krugman' and 'khakis' is strong — Krugman's New York Times brand as measured liberal intellectual makes the vulgarity funnier. Richard's guileless delivery of crude language is a reliable character beat.

jokes.text: What state are you a delegate from? / The Ukraine. / A Wisconsin sister state.
jokes.setup: A delegate wants a photo with Richard after his speech goes viral.
jokes.punchline: Richard has apparently made such an impression that a Ukrainian delegate is present, and a bystander calls Ukraine 'a Wisconsin sister state' with complete authority.
jokes.explanation: Quick absurdist gag. The Ukraine/Wisconsin comparison is an inspired non-sequitur that lands because it's delivered with the confidence of someone who believes it completely. The chaos of Richard's sudden celebrity is well-captured in the rush of photo requests.

jokes.text: Why does everybody get good at their job after I fire them?
jokes.setup: Selina learns that Leon (her former press secretary) is working with Kemi and digging up dirt on the Meyer Fund.
jokes.punchline: Selina's complaint is a self-own — she's essentially admitting her management of talent is the problem.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. Perfectly economical, deeply character-specific, and works as genuine self-awareness that Selina immediately fails to process. The irony is rich — she's the obstacle to these people's competence, and she's framing it as their betrayal. Strong contender for the episode's best line.

jokes.text: I would rather cheese-grater my clit.
jokes.setup: Ben is quoting Eleanor Roosevelt to convince Selina to make Kemi her Veep.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — attributed to Eleanor Roosevelt — is the most visceral possible rejection.
jokes.explanation: The setup ('In the words of the great Eleanor Roosevelt') creates a perfect expectation of a dignified quote, then detonates it with something spectacularly vulgar. The joke is also Selina claiming this as Eleanor Roosevelt's words, which adds a layer of absurdism. Extremely quotable, great economy.

jokes.text: Oh, no, ma'am. I'm a cis woman. / Right. That's not a crazy question to ask. / Not at all.
jokes.setup: Selina thanks 'Peppermint Patty' (Marjorie) for the trans community's support, clearly thinking Marjorie is trans.
jokes.punchline: Marjorie corrects her, and both Selina and Catherine immediately rationalize Selina's mistake ('That's not a crazy question to ask').
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed cringe beat — Selina's casual bigotry-as-assumption, covered over by the sycophantic rationalization. The 'Peppermint Patty' nickname for Marjorie (the androgynous-looking character) is the real payload.

jokes.text: You and your gay pals can make me the grand marshal of the next parade that you have that messes up all the traffic.
jokes.setup: Catherine is celebrating Selina's accidental trans-rights stand.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'reward' framing — treating becoming grand marshal of a pride parade as a favor she's granting, and the specific annoyance about traffic — perfectly captures her selfish condescension.
jokes.explanation: A precise Selina character beat — her 'support' for marginalized groups is always self-serving and laced with casual disdain. 'That messes up all the traffic' is the perfect Selina complaint, treating Pride as primarily a commute inconvenience.

jokes.text: Actually, ma'am, it's a myth that lesbians and gay men have any natural affinity. As a matter of fact, Gertrude Stein was physically disgusted by male homosexuals. / Excuse me? / How'd she feel about tedious lesbians? / Big fan, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina refers to 'your gay pals' to Marjorie (Catherine's lesbian partner).
jokes.punchline: Marjorie delivers an unsolicited Gertrude Stein lecture; Selina pivots with 'tedious lesbians'; Marjorie confirms Stein was a 'big fan' — a perfectly deadpan self-description.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The structure is impeccable: Marjorie's pedantic Stein lecture is already funny, Selina's 'How'd she feel about tedious lesbians?' is a devastating comeback, and Marjorie's 'Big fan, ma'am' — completely deadpan, no indication she recognizes she's calling herself tedious — is the perfect button. The triple-beat structure, the historical specificity, and Marjorie's unflappable self-unawareness make this exceptional. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: This is why everyone should just fly private.
jokes.setup: Richard alerts Selina that the NYPD found a van full of explosives at Kennedy Airport, and that she's been knocked out of the news cycle.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to a terrorist bomb plot is a complaint about not flying private.
jokes.explanation: Eight words that encapsulate Selina's entire character — oblivious to human suffering, focused on status signaling, and completely tone-deaf about optics. The economy is flawless. Her concern is that she's been knocked out of the news cycle by the bombing, not the bomb itself. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: The president, she loved your speech. Especially the line 'There are really three Americas.' / I'm so glad you responded to that. / She's gonna use that in her speech.
jokes.setup: Kent is managing DeVito while Selina uses him as a prop to avoid conversation.
jokes.punchline: Kent is systematically stripping DeVito's speech of its best lines for Selina's use, framed as compliments.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed running piece of cringe — the cruelty of complimenting DeVito on lines they're simultaneously stealing from him. Kent's 'She'll tweak it' is the perfect bureaucratic euphemism for plagiarism.

jokes.text: The most compelling number to me is not a number at all, it's a concept. / I said pretend, okay? / I was pretending. My favorite number is Euler's number.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Kent to 'pretend to talk to me about numbers' as a ruse to avoid DeVito.
jokes.punchline: Kent immediately goes so deep into genuine mathematical philosophy that he has to be corrected, then reveals his actual favorite number is Euler's number — indicating he wasn't pretending at all.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Kent character comedy. His relationship with numbers and data is so deep that 'pretending' to talk about numbers is indistinguishable from actual passion. Euler's number (e ≈ 2.718) is the perfect specific detail — it confirms Kent's genuine mathematical enthusiasm and complete inability to perform normalcy. Near-perfect character integration.

jokes.text: She's also gonna take the line 'No matter what, I will always stand for America.' / That's a reference to my leg. / She'll tweak it.
jokes.setup: Kent is continuing to strip lines from DeVito's speech.
jokes.punchline: DeVito notes the line is a personal reference to his prosthetic leg (he 'stands' for America literally), and Kent dismisses it with 'She'll tweak it' — implying Selina will remove the disability reference that gives the line its meaning.
jokes.explanation: The darkest beat in the DeVito sequence — stealing a line that was literally about a veteran's prosthetic leg, then promising to 'tweak' the personal meaning out of it. 'She'll tweak it' is doing enormous work as a button line.

jokes.text: I want to offer Buddy Calhoun Veep. / Okay. / 'Cause with his delegates and all that Bible-y BS, the rest of the delegates'll have to fall in line, and Kemi can go and suck lecture-circuit dick in hell. And by 'hell,' I mean The Kennedy School.
jokes.setup: Selina immediately reverses course on her Veep pick (DeVito) to pivot to Calhoun.
jokes.punchline: 'Kemi can go suck lecture-circuit dick in hell' is already good; 'And by hell, I mean The Kennedy School' is the perfect button — implying Harvard's Kennedy School is indistinguishable from hell for a politician.
jokes.explanation: The Kennedy School button is sharp insider satire — the idea that prestigious academic appointments after a political loss are actually worse than hell resonates perfectly. 'Bible-y BS' is also a good efficient phrase.

jokes.text: Oh, please. Buddy's not flirting with anything that's not wearing a leather vest and a Nazi cap.
jokes.setup: Staffers warn Selina that Buddy Calhoun is flirting with endorsing Kemi.
jokes.punchline: Selina's confident assessment of Buddy's type — implying he's a closeted gay man with a very specific leather aesthetic.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Calhoun payoff later in the episode. 'Leather vest and Nazi cap' is a specific enough image to be funny — it places him in a very defined subculture. The rewatch value is high because this offhand line turns out to be exactly right.

jokes.text: God created men's rooms and women's rooms, not 'everyone rooms'. / I don't want to go into a bathroom and be next to a woman who is looking at my genitals. I want that person that I'm next to, to be a man, or, better yet, lots of men. / Glory, glory, hole-elujah.
jokes.setup: Buddy Calhoun is making his anti-trans bathroom argument to reporters.
jokes.punchline: His specific desire to be watched by men ('lots of men') and Gary's 'Glory, glory, hole-elujah' pun make clear that Buddy's objection reveals his actual desires.
jokes.explanation: Extraordinarily well-constructed. The three-beat structure builds perfectly: anti-trans position → specific preference for lots of men → 'Glory, glory, hole-elujah.' The pun is Gary's best moment of the series — 'hole-elujah' is a wordplay hat trick (hallelujah + glory hole + Buddy's sexuality). The anti-gay politician being revealed as gay through his own words is a classic satirical move, but executed here with unusual precision.

jokes.text: You came to the convention as Governor Richard Splett of Iowa, but after your electrifying speech just two nights ago, party insiders are calling you Richard Splett, 'future of the party.'
jokes.setup: Mike is interviewing Richard on CBS News.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of Richard Splett — the most earnest, incompetent-seeming governor — being called the 'future of the party' is inherently funny to anyone who knows the character.
jokes.explanation: Situational comedy — the humor comes from the contrast between Richard's actual person and his new political status. Works as setup for Richard's comedic interview beats more than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: When you listen to rumors, you rue more than... Nope. I don't know where I was going with that. / Works in sign language.
jokes.setup: Mike asks Richard about rumors that Senator Talbot is talking to him about a position.
jokes.punchline: Richard starts a would-be witty deflection, loses the thread mid-sentence, admits it, then notes it 'works in sign language' — an incredibly specific callback to his ASL skills mentioned moments earlier.
jokes.explanation: Richard's best moment in the episode. The incomplete aphorism is funny on its own ('I don't know where I was going with that' is a great deadpan confession), but 'Works in sign language' — the immediate callback to ASL skills mentioned 20 seconds earlier — elevates this to excellent. The joke implies he's simultaneously terrible with words and thinks signing the incomplete phrase makes it coherent. Perfect Richard character comedy.

jokes.text: Twenty-Five Things That No One Knows About You. / Well, number one is, I love lists.
jokes.setup: Us Weekly wants to do a piece on Richard.
jokes.punchline: Richard's first unknown fact about himself is that he 'loves lists' — a completely banal meta-response to being asked for a list.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple and very well-constructed. The meta-joke (his answer to a list is that he loves lists) combined with the complete banality of the revelation ('I love lists') and Richard's earnest delivery make this a small gem. Economy is exceptional — it's a perfect joke in 12 words.

jokes.text: Have Jonah instruct his delegates of Dr. Moreau to back me on the second ballot tonight, and we might be able to find something for him at... / EPA. / Perfect.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to throw Jonah a bone in exchange for delegates.
jokes.punchline: 'Delegates of Dr. Moreau' (implying Jonah's backers are monstrous human-animal hybrids); Ben immediately lands on EPA as the place to do least harm; Selina's 'Perfect' confirms she chose EPA specifically to neutralize Jonah.
jokes.explanation: 'Delegates of Dr. Moreau' is a strong literary insult for Jonah's constituency. The EPA beat — choosing it as where Jonah can do the least harm, which under a Republican-ish administration likely means Jonah would dismantle it — has insider-politics irony.

jokes.text: The suspect in the JFK bombing is one Mr. Abdul Azeem, a high school mathematics teacher whose Facebook page is all about radical Islam and trigonometry. / So are a lot of people's.
jokes.setup: A terrorist bombing suspect has been identified.
jokes.punchline: Amy's deadpan defense of Facebook pages devoted to 'radical Islam and trigonometry' as being common — which is both absurd and a callback to Jonah's anti-math campaign.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Jonah's anti-math crusade pays off enormously here — a mathematician IS the bomber, making Jonah spectacularly correct. Amy's deadpan 'so are a lot of people's' is both funny as a non-response and exposes how absurd it is that Jonah was right.

jokes.text: Jonah was right. He was spectacularly right! And you know what that makes me, sister? / Spitty? / The campaign manager for the next President of the United States!
jokes.setup: Amy is excited that the math bomber confirms Jonah's anti-math rhetoric.
jokes.punchline: The build to 'the campaign manager for the next President' is punctured by 'Spitty?' — a reference to Amy's habit of spitting when excited — and then Amy doesn't even acknowledge the interjection.
jokes.explanation: 'Spitty?' as the interjection during Amy's triumphant moment is a characteristic Veep move — the physical comedy of Amy's excitement being the joke instead of what she's excited about. The fact that she ignores it completely is the button.

jokes.text: For the last year, I have been crisscrossing the country, warning America about the threat of math to our way of life. / Yeah! / Which is why, 'President Meyer, Senator Talbot, and Governor Calhoun,' I have something to say to you. I told you so!
jokes.setup: Jonah addresses the candidates at the convention following the JFK bombing by a math teacher.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's years-long, deeply stupid anti-math crusade has been validated by actual events, and his 'I told you so' is genuinely triumphant — and genuinely insane.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of Jonah's season-long joke — his most absurd policy position being vindicated by a genuine terrorist is a perfect Veep satirical move about how random events validate stupid politics. Character integration is high because only Jonah's arc makes this work.

jokes.text: Do you remember my five-alarm hottie of a wife, Beth? She just got out of rehab today. Yeah! And plus, while she was in there, she dropped a couple pounds in all the right places, so, she's hella even hotter than she was before. / Kill her! / Yeah! I mean, not her, man, but yeah, sure.
jokes.setup: Jonah is triumphantly rallying his supporters after his vindication.
jokes.punchline: Uncle Jeff's reflexive 'Kill her!' in response to Beth's attractiveness, then walking it back slightly ('not her, man, but yeah, sure') — the half-retraction being funnier than the impulse.
jokes.explanation: Jeff's 'Kill her!' is pure id — the show's way of communicating the genuine violence simmering under political excitement. The 'not her, man, but yeah, sure' is the correct button — Jeff's instincts are murder, but he has enough social awareness to specify the target.

jokes.text: Look, I love America, but it is time to face facts. This is a horrific country that is falling apart because it is full of people who are different than me.
jokes.setup: Jonah is giving his victory speech.
jokes.punchline: 'I love America, but it is full of people who are different than me' — the complete logic of nationalist populism condensed to its essential absurdity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best political satire lines. The logic — 'I love America, but it's full of people different from me' — is a perfect reduction of white nationalist populism to its most honest and absurd core. It's also a perfect Jonah line: he lacks the self-awareness to understand he's accidentally articulating the problem clearly. 'Full of people who are different than me' as the entire complaint is devastating satire. High quotability, strong structure.

jokes.text: I thought you told my mom to sell me to a child molester and use the money to get her tubes tied. / Eh, we're family. Come here, you!
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff is warmly reminiscing about believing in Jonah since he was little.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's matter-of-fact correction that Jeff told his mother to sell him to a child molester, and Jeff's dismissive 'Eh, we're family' as if this is normal family ribbing.
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally dark and perfectly structured. The setup (Uncle Jeff's heartwarming speech) is completely undercut by Jonah's flat correction, which is more devastating because it's so specific (sell to child molester + use money for tubal ligation). Jeff's 'Eh, we're family' and the immediate hug treat this as normal, which is the perfect button. Character-specific and quotable.

jokes.text: You know, the Jews have a word for this feeling I'm having right now. I can't remember it. It sounds like three Germans cumming real hard inside something that doesn't want them to. / Oh, Jewish is such a beautiful language.
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff is trying to describe his joy at Jonah's success.
jokes.punchline: The phonetic description of the unknown Yiddish word — 'three Germans cumming real hard inside something that doesn't want them to' — is both antisemitic (in character) and accidentally historically resonant in the worst way; Jonah's 'Jewish is such a beautiful language' confirms neither knows what they're doing.
jokes.explanation: Dark, uncomfortable, and carefully written. The joke works by layering: Jeff is reaching for Yiddish (likely schadenfreude) but can only describe it through violent sexual imagery involving Germans — a combination that is historically horrifying and reveals his ignorance and casual bigotry. Jonah's straight-faced 'Jewish is such a beautiful language' confirms neither character has processed what was just said. High craft in the construction of awfulness.

jokes.text: Listen, you Subaru-driving bull dykes! You better back Jonah on the next ballot, or you will be leaf-peeping from inside your own maple syrup-soaked assholes!
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff is trying to sweet-talk the Vermont delegation into supporting Jonah.
jokes.punchline: The rapid escalation from 'sweet-talk' to 'Subaru-driving bull dykes' to the maple syrup threat — and the Vermont-specific specificity of both the insults and the imagery.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between 'I think I can sweet-talk them' and the immediate detonation into maximum offensive territory. The Vermont specificity ('Subaru,' 'bull dykes,' 'leaf-peeping,' 'maple syrup') shows craft — these aren't random insults but regionally targeted ones. 'I'll give 'em five minutes, then I'll hit 'em with bad cop' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Assemblyman, I have been to Buffalo six times, and I'm not even a serial killer. So I... Okay, forget it.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to woo a New York assemblyman by demonstrating her commitment to the state.
jokes.punchline: Her argument that she's not a serial killer — as the proof of her dedication to Buffalo — collapses mid-sentence when she realizes it's not compelling.
jokes.explanation: The logic — 'I visit Buffalo voluntarily and I'm not a serial killer, therefore I'm committed' — only works if you think Buffalo is so terrible that voluntarily visiting it is a meaningful sacrifice. The self-aware abandonment of the argument ('Okay, forget it') is the right call and makes the joke land cleanly.

jokes.text: And just tell 'em they can violate me with their assault rifles and a full slab of ribs on top of a stack of slavery-free history textbooks.
jokes.setup: The Texas delegation is moving toward Jonah; Ben needs to win them back.
jokes.punchline: Selina's offer to Texas combines sexual violence, guns, BBQ, and the specific Texas political issue of history textbooks (censorship of slavery content) — a complete satirical map of Texas political pandering.
jokes.explanation: The specificity is doing the work here — 'slavery-free history textbooks' is a real Texas political issue, and stacking it underneath sexual violence and BBQ creates a satirical portrait of Texas electoral pandering that's more pointed than a list of generic red-state references.

jokes.text: I just wanna be president. / Do you want six almonds? / No!
jokes.setup: Selina is in existential despair about the Meyer Fund investigation and Kemi's attacks.
jokes.punchline: Gary's response to Selina's deepest emotional vulnerability is to offer her six almonds — his role as her body-servant overriding everything. Her sharp 'No!' is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy in three lines. 'I just wanna be president' is Selina at her most nakedly honest — a rare moment of vulnerability — and Gary immediately responds with his function: food management. 'Six almonds' is the specific detail that makes it hilarious (not just 'some almonds'). Economy is flawless. This is Gary and Selina's relationship in miniature.

jokes.text: This, Kent, is why I was right not to offer her Veep. / She's the worst. / Yeah. / Ma'am, all due respect, maybe you should consider being her Veep.
jokes.setup: Selina and Kent are watching Kemi announce the FBI investigation into the Meyer Fund on TV.
jokes.punchline: Kent agrees Kemi is the worst, then immediately suggests Selina become Kemi's Veep — the most insulting possible suggestion, delivered in Kent's flat analytical tone.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the pure Kent delivery — he genuinely concurs with 'She's the worst,' then in the next breath suggests Selina subordinate herself to that person. Kent's complete affect-free pragmatism makes this devastatingly funny. 'That's not fucking funny, Kent' is exactly the right response, and his 'I haven't been funny since 1987' is a perfect button.

jokes.text: That's not fucking funny, Kent. / I haven't been funny since 1987.
jokes.setup: Selina reacts furiously to Kent's suggestion she be Kemi's Veep.
jokes.punchline: Kent's confirmation that he wasn't trying to be funny — and the specific year 1987 as the last time he was.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single lines. 'I haven't been funny since 1987' accomplishes several things simultaneously: it confirms Kent's total self-awareness about his personality, the specific year suggests something happened in 1987 that ended his sense of humor (left to the imagination), and it's delivered completely deadpan, which is why it's funny. This is the purest Kent character comedy in the episode — ten words that contain an entire character history.

jokes.text: Adios, New Mexico.
jokes.setup: The team is watching the news and receiving bad news about delegates.
jokes.punchline: A flat farewell to the New Mexico delegation, delivered as a brief sign-off to a relationship ending.
jokes.explanation: Quick throwaway gag — the informal farewell to a state (like saying goodbye to a person) is a minor absurdist beat. Works as comic punctuation in a scene that needs levity.

jokes.text: Oh, yes! Politico's reporting that Jonah's talking to you about the Secretary of Commerce. / I wish. Sounds much better than the conversation we were having about why his urine is pink. / We ruled out beets because he doesn't know what those are. / Then had to rule it right back in for the same reason.
jokes.setup: Selina is using a fake Politico story about Richard and Jonah to leverage him.
jokes.punchline: Richard's escalating revelation about Jonah's urine — ruling out beets because he doesn't know what they are, then ruling them back in for the same reason — is a perfect three-beat medical comedy.
jokes.explanation: Arguably the funniest sustained joke in the episode. The structure is impeccable: setup (pink urine), beat 1 (ruled out beets — he doesn't know what they are), button (had to rule them back in for the same reason — he might eat them without knowing). The logic is airtight and devastating. 'For the same reason' is the perfect closing line — it implies Jonah is so ignorant he might be eating beets accidentally. Character-specific to Jonah's bottomless stupidity, and Richard delivers it with his trademark guileless sincerity. This is the episode's best-structured joke.

jokes.text: You could've ended that sentence at 'me.'
jokes.setup: Selina says 'It was me, all right? I pulled that turd out of my ass, covered it in ketchup, and Politico asked for seconds.'
jokes.punchline: Richard's suggestion that 'It was me' was sufficient without the subsequent metaphor — delivered without judgment, just editorial precision.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and structure. Eight words that simultaneously comment on Selina's excessive metaphor-making, reveal Richard's editorial sensibility, and work as a straight-faced critique of Veep's own brand of profanity-dense dialogue. Richard as unintentional comedy commentator is a strong character beat.

jokes.text: All right, listen, one thing is clear after this runaway-Ferris wheel of a convention, and that's that Montez is gonna be president for another four years. / Makes sense. She made me proud to be an American again. / Okay, but after that, it's Splett time. / We can't call it that unless we want to get sued by my uncle's podcast.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to set up a long-game political alliance with Richard.
jokes.punchline: 'Splett time' as a slogan is undermined by Richard's uncle having already trademarked it for a podcast — which also implies 'Splett time' is a phrase that exists naturally in Richard's family universe.
jokes.explanation: Richard's casual correction has great specificity — 'my uncle's podcast' implies a whole world of Splett family enterprises. Also, Richard completely ignores the insulting Montez remark and responds to it positively, which is a good character beat.

jokes.text: Eighth time's the charm.
jokes.setup: Ben appears to be having a heart attack.
jokes.punchline: Someone in the room reacts to Ben's heart attack with 'Eighth time's the charm' — implying this is his eighth, and whoever said it knows his medical history well enough to count.
jokes.explanation: A five-word joke that lands hard. The inversion of 'third time's the charm' to eighth is already funny; the implication that everyone in the room is so familiar with Ben's cardiac events that they track the count is character comedy for the whole ensemble.

jokes.text: White male, type 2 diabetes... also type 1.
jokes.setup: Gary is relaying Ben's medical information to the EMTs.
jokes.punchline: The addition of 'also type 1' diabetes — suggesting Ben has both types simultaneously — is medically absurd but delivered with the confidence of someone reading from a card.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'also type 1' is where the joke lives — the beat of recollection and addition. Gary knowing this level of medical detail and the absurdity of having both types of diabetes is a solid medical escalation joke.

jokes.text: You know, I was roadkill on the side of the political highway. One of the nice things about being dead is, it gives you time to think. Also, fewer telemarketers.
jokes.setup: Tom James is addressing the crowd that's calling for him to enter the race.
jokes.punchline: 'Also, fewer telemarketers' as an upside of political death — a completely unexpected mundane practical benefit tagged onto a political metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Tom James's self-deprecating wit is well-calibrated here. The speech begins with a reasonable political metaphor, then the 'also, fewer telemarketers' misdirection perfectly deploys the mundane to undercut the grandiosity. Character-specific to Tom's dry wit.

jokes.text: I honestly think I might be in hell. / No such place, ma'am. The concept of hell is a cultural memory of pre-Mosaic child sacrifice among proto-Judean peoples. / You're making a strong case for hell.
jokes.setup: Selina is watching Tom James's surge and despair.
jokes.punchline: Kent's pedantic theological correction becomes its own argument for hell's existence, which Selina immediately weaponizes.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional three-line exchange. Kent's compulsive pedantry — correcting 'I'm in hell' with detailed theological anthropology — is perfect character comedy, and Selina's comeback ('You're making a strong case for hell') is one of the best lines of the episode: it's funny as a literal reversal (his correction proves hell exists) and as an insult (listening to Kent IS hell). The structure is textbook: setup, unexpected subversion, reversal. Near-perfect.

jokes.text: My first congressional race. Ben can't have been more than 140 years old. He ran the other guy, wiped the floor with me.
jokes.setup: Tom James is visiting Ben in the hospital, recalling their history.
jokes.punchline: 'Ben can't have been more than 140 years old' — a hyperbolic assessment of Ben's ancient appearance, delivered with the fondness of a genuine compliment.
jokes.explanation: A warm insult that works because it comes from Tom during what is genuinely a touching scene. The hyperbole (140) is the right scale — it's absurd enough to be funny while affectionately describing Ben's ancient quality.

jokes.text: Well, they may have to put him in a medically induced coma, so... / It's what he always wanted.
jokes.setup: Selina updates Tom on Ben's medical condition.
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'It's what he always wanted' — treating medically induced coma as a dream fulfilled.
jokes.explanation: Five words that simultaneously eulogize Ben and make the audience laugh. The joke works because it's likely true — given what we know of Ben's exhaustion — and Tom's fond delivery makes it moving rather than cruel. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Well, it's been fun. Let's do this again at Ben's next cardiopulmonary event. / I'll wave at you from the stage.
jokes.setup: Tom James is leaving after making an implicit political threat.
jokes.punchline: Scheduling the next meeting around Ben's next cardiac event, then the casual 'I'll wave at you from the stage' — a victory lap delivered before victory.
jokes.explanation: Two good beats — the casual scheduling of a meeting around Ben's expected future cardiac event is dark and specific, and 'I'll wave at you from the stage' is a confident political threat delivered with Tom's characteristic pleasant cruelty.

jokes.text: Wipe that grin-eating dick off your face.
jokes.setup: A staffer/Ben is visibly amused by Tom's speech/exit.
jokes.punchline: The malapropism/corruption of 'shit-eating grin' into 'grin-eating dick' — a Freudian scramble that says more than the original.
jokes.explanation: Inspired wordplay/malapropism. 'Grin-eating dick' is a corruption of 'shit-eating grin' that's both funnier and more Selina — the aggression is redirected and the image is more obscene. The scramble feels unintentional (which is funnier) and the line is instantly quotable. Strong economy, excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Tom James. / Kent says he'll take it on tomorrow night's ballot. / I think this is it for me, too. / Oh, no. You're gonna be fine. You're gonna be good as new, and then we'll figure out my next move.
jokes.setup: Ben and Selina are having what may be their final conversation; Ben implies he won't survive.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'comfort' is immediately about herself — 'we'll figure out my next move' — revealing her inability to be present for someone else's mortality.
jokes.explanation: Bittersweet character comedy — the joke is in how Selina turns a deathbed conversation back to her own political future. It's played more for pathos than laughs, but the instinctive self-absorption is very funny and very true to the character.

jokes.text: Hey, do me a favor. / What? / Don't tell my wife and kids, okay? I mean, I could really use the break. / That's not a problem. I've never met 'em.
jokes.setup: Ben is lying in the hospital, ostensibly having a final exchange with Selina.
jokes.punchline: Ben asks Selina not to tell his family about his condition — because he 'could use a break' from them. Selina's button: she's never met them.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best moment. The structure is flawless: Ben's deathbed request — not to be heroic or sentimental, but to get a break from his family — is perfectly Ben. Then Selina's 'That's not a problem. I've never met 'em' delivers two simultaneous payoffs: she's confirming the favor is easy, AND revealing that despite decades of working together, she's never met his family — which tells you everything about both characters and their relationship. This is the 'Snip snap' caliber of Veep writing: dark, perfectly structured, economical, deeply character-specific, and utterly quotable.

jokes.text: Tom's making a move on New York, so, you get ahold of Governor Schnozzlestein right now...
jokes.setup: Selina is rallying after Ben's hospitalization, refusing to give up.
jokes.punchline: 'Governor Schnozzlestein' — an offhand antisemitic nickname for a New York governor delivered as a matter-of-fact bureaucratic instruction.
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat — Selina's casual bigotry in her private vocabulary. 'Schnozzlestein' as a composite antisemitic trope (big nose + Jewish name suffix) is dark but the show has done this type of gag better elsewhere. It's functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Can't say I blame you. I mean, that Nutmeg State indefinable really turns my hydrant on.
jokes.setup: An aide makes a comment implying something suggestive about the Connecticut/New York governor connection.
jokes.punchline: 'Turns my hydrant on' as a sexual metaphor, paired with 'Nutmeg State' — a very specific CT reference.
jokes.explanation: A minor joke. 'Turns my hydrant on' is an acceptable Veep-style crude euphemism and the Nutmeg State reference is specific, but the joke doesn't build to anything particularly sharp.

jokes.text: ...trust me, he will never see you as anything other than the TGI Friday's hostess on Proactiv who lets him bend you over his desk, while you close your eyes to avoid coming face-to-face with that framed photo of his family's trip to Aspen, while he drowns your Little Mermaid back tat in a pool of jizz and admires his own reflection.
jokes.setup: Selina is warning Tom James's female staffer/chief of staff about her relationship with Tom.
jokes.punchline: The extended, escalating, hyper-specific portrait of a political affair — from TGI Friday's to Proactiv to Little Mermaid tattoo — is both an insult and a pitch-perfect satirical portrait of DC power dynamics.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary extended insult that earns its length through escalating specificity. Each detail adds: TGI Friday's (working class), Proactiv (acne, youth), Aspen family photo (his actual life), Little Mermaid back tat (naivety), mirror (his narcissism). Selina delivering it with a concerned feminist veneer ('I just hate to see smart women throw away their political careers') adds the final layer of hypocrisy. Economy score is lower because it runs long, but it earns its length.

jokes.text: I just hate to see smart women throw away their political careers on powerful men who only see them as the gash of least resistance. / Mmm. / I mean, you strike me as a smart woman. / Are you?
jokes.setup: Selina finishes her extended insult/warning with a feminist framing.
jokes.punchline: 'Gash of least resistance' as a feminist reframe of 'path of least resistance' — and the entire speech being a manipulation rather than genuine support.
jokes.explanation: 'Gash of least resistance' is an inspired wordplay — it takes a neutral idiom and makes it specifically vulgar/political. The irony of Selina performing feminism as a manipulation tactic is very sharp Veep writing about how feminist rhetoric gets weaponized.

jokes.text: I was just this naive, innocent young woman who had dreams... / 'Innocent young woman.' That's hilarious, don't you think? / But that girl doesn't exist anymore. She's dead. / Murdered by Tom James. / That is actually all me. I will take credit for that.
jokes.setup: Tom James's aide is giving a tearful statement about sexual harassment by Tom James — a hit job orchestrated by Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina gleefully commenting on the performance she engineered — 'That girl doesn't exist anymore. She's dead. Murdered by Tom James.' / 'That is actually all me. I will take credit for that.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest comedy-cruelty moments. Selina watching her own political assassination unfold on TV and literally taking authorial credit for the best lines ('that is actually all me') while condemning Tom is both funny and chilling. Her pride in the writing quality of the hit job is perfectly Selina.

jokes.text: My wife, my pregnant wife, is not answering the phone. I am now a national fucking pariah! Why would you do this, Selina? / I guess that the Tom James charm only goes so far with the ladies. / That's one theory, but... Oh, listen, while I've got you. Is it okay if I don't wave to you from the stage? Because with all of this, I need to keep my distance.
jokes.setup: Tom James has just been destroyed by the sexual harassment hit job Selina orchestrated.
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual 'Is it okay if I don't wave to you from the stage?' — a callback to Tom's earlier threat — now delivered as a considerate scheduling request while he's in ruins.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure. Tom's 'I'll wave at you from the stage' was a confident victory taunt; Selina reversing it — asking permission NOT to wave, framed as a considerate distance-keeping measure — is perfectly executed reversal comedy. The polite phrasing ('Is it okay if I don't wave to you') while he's being destroyed is peak Selina cruelty-as-manners. One of the best callbacks in the episode.

jokes.text: Toodle-oo. / So emotional, right? This is why we need a woman in office.
jokes.setup: Tom James has just stormed off in distress.
jokes.punchline: Selina waves him off with 'Toodle-oo' and then frames the emotional man's meltdown as an argument for female leadership.
jokes.explanation: 'Toodle-oo' is perfectly infantilizing. The 'This is why we need a woman in office' line is a masterpiece of hypocrisy — Selina has just committed multiple crimes to destroy a man, and now invokes feminist governance norms. The inversion of the sexist trope (the emotional man used to argue for female leadership) is sharp.

jokes.text: Governor Calhoun, what do I have to do to get your support? / President Meyer, I'm a simple man... / I see that.
jokes.setup: Selina is negotiating with Buddy Calhoun for his endorsement.
jokes.punchline: Buddy begins with 'I'm a simple man' and Selina immediately agrees — 'I see that' — with complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Three-word comeback that lands perfectly. 'I see that' as a sincere agreement with an insult-embedded compliment is a strong Selina move. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Pernicious homosexuals are luring decent, God-fearing heterosexual men into sin and sweaty degradation. / Mmm. / You know, Buddy, I gotta tell you something. It... It happens, and... / You can't beat yourself up about it. / What?
jokes.setup: Buddy is making his standard anti-gay speech to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — 'It happens, and you can't beat yourself up about it' — is a knowing, sympathetic acknowledgment of gay sex directed at the anti-gay politician who is clearly gay.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff to the earlier leather vest line. Selina's gentle acknowledgment — 'It happens, you can't beat yourself up about it' — reads as genuine pastoral support to someone who's expressed something she instinctively understands about him. Buddy's confused 'What?' is perfect. The scene then pays off with the deal: Selina will ban gay marriage to secure his endorsement despite knowing he's gay, which adds a dark satirical layer.

jokes.text: I'll kill gay marriage. / In exchange for your support, I will raw-dog a plank right up our party's platform, and I will outlaw same-sex marriage.
jokes.setup: Buddy is expressing concern about the Meyer Fund, and Selina is looking for a way to secure his endorsement.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll kill gay marriage' as an offhand offer, then the 'raw-dog a plank' metaphor — offering to violate the party platform sexually to outlaw same-sex marriage.
jokes.explanation: The casual 'I'll kill gay marriage' — offered like tossing in a free dessert — is the key beat. 'Raw-dog a plank right up our party's platform' is an inspired metaphor for forcing through a policy plank, darkened by the implications of doing this specifically to benefit a closeted gay man.

jokes.text: Governor, can I get an amen? / You can get an amen, and I'll throw in a hallelujah. / I love it. / And my endorsement. / Thank you, sir. Wonderful! / Wow. Yeah. / I think we're buddies now, aren't we? / Oh, yeah, very much. / Can you be a buddy with someone of the opposite sex? / Oh, well, under certain circumstances, yeah, we are. / Buddy up! Buddy up! / That's a great expression.
jokes.setup: The deal has been struck — Selina will outlaw same-sex marriage, Buddy endorses her.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Buddy up!' pun on his name/friendship, and the running absurdity of Buddy wondering whether a man and woman can be 'buddies' — a theological question given Buddy's likely closeted status.
jokes.explanation: The scene-closer works on the name pun ('Buddy up') and on Buddy's sincere theological questioning of male-female friendship, which reads differently given what we know of him. Not the episode's sharpest work but a functional closer.

jokes.text: He is not just gay, he is Sam Rayburn gay.
jokes.setup: Kent is observing Buddy Calhoun immediately after Selina's negotiation.
jokes.punchline: Kent's precise calibration of Buddy's closeted homosexuality — not just gay, but 'Sam Rayburn gay' (Rayburn was the longtime House Speaker widely rumored to be closeted).
jokes.explanation: The episode's best-crafted joke. Kent deploying a historically specific political gay reference (Sam Rayburn, Speaker of the House 1940-1961, famously closeted) to calibrate the degree of Buddy's homosexuality is perfect Kent character comedy — he would know exactly who Sam Rayburn was and use him as a unit of measurement. The economy is flawless (nine words), the historical specificity is exceptional, and the character integration is as high as it gets — only Kent would make this comparison, delivered with zero affect. This rivals the anchors.

jokes.text: Not in that outfit. But yeah, come on in.
jokes.setup: Gary announces Amy's arrival and Selina says 'You wanted to see me?'
jokes.punchline: 'Not in that outfit' — Selina's instinctive fashion judgment before the political consideration.
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat — Selina's vanity/snobbery operating as a reflex. The humor is in the priority inversion: she's about to ask Amy to help with the most important decision of her career, and her first reaction is a fashion critique.

jokes.text: You can't let an embittered, vindictive, narcissistic man-child be one heartbeat away from the presidency, let alone be the president. / Amy, there's no safer place to stick Jonah Ryan in all of Washington, D.C. Being vice president is like being declawed, defanged, neutered, ball-gagged, and sealed in an abandoned coal mine under two miles of human shit! It is a fate worse than death.
jokes.setup: Amy is begging Selina not to make Jonah VP.
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating list of what the Vice Presidency does to a person — 'declawed, defanged, neutered, ball-gagged, sealed in an abandoned coal mine under two miles of human shit' — culminating in 'fate worse than death.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating list is a Veep specialty and this is one of the better deployments — each item in the list escalates from veterinary (declawed, defanged, neutered) to sexual (ball-gagged) to industrial-gothic (sealed in coal mine under human shit). The structure mirrors the progression of someone getting increasingly desperate to make their point. Strong character integration — Selina would know this from personal experience as VP.

jokes.text: Besides, I'm not gonna die, 'cause I got the heart and the twat of a high school cheerleader who's only done anal!
jokes.setup: Selina is dismissing Amy's concerns about her dying and Jonah becoming president.
jokes.punchline: The specific physical metaphor — a cheerleader who's 'only done anal' as a symbol of cardiovascular fitness — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The cheerleader metaphor for Selina's fitness is pure Veep — the specific qualifier ('only done anal,' implying preserved cardiovascular innocence) is the kind of disgusting non-sequitur that the show deploys better than anyone. Highly quotable, strong character integration.

jokes.text: She is offering you vice president, you monument to vaginal dryness.
jokes.setup: Jonah doesn't understand why he's been summoned to Selina's suite.
jokes.punchline: 'Monument to vaginal dryness' as an insult — implying Jonah is the physical embodiment of sexual repulsion.
jokes.explanation: 'Monument to vaginal dryness' is an exceptional compound insult — 'monument' elevates it to something permanent and architectural, which combined with the sexual repulsion imagery creates an image both vivid and absurd. Highly quotable, strong craft.

jokes.text: Well, then, no. I'm sorry? I said no. As in never. I will be president, or I will be nothing.
jokes.setup: Jeff tells Jonah he's being offered the Vice Presidency.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's flat refusal — 'I will be president, or I will be nothing' — is funny because it's both deluded and, within the world of the show, approaching political reality.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's grandiosity is well-calibrated here — the joke is that he's turned down a genuinely attractive offer on principle, which is both stupid and somehow admirable. The 'as in never' clarification is a good Jonah touch.

jokes.text: Shut the fuck up, you gum-recessed face-anus! Don't you see you've just been offered the second-most powerful job in the world? / No, you shut up, Uncle Jeff! I will not let anyone speak to me like that. / President or nothing? / Yeah! / Are you fucking kidding me? / Ow!
jokes.setup: Jeff and Jonah are arguing about the VP offer.
jokes.punchline: 'Gum-recessed face-anus' as an insult; then the escalation to apparent physical violence ('Ow!') — Jeff presumably hitting Jonah.
jokes.explanation: 'Gum-recessed face-anus' is an extraordinary compound insult — anatomically precise ('gum-recessed'), then the 'face-anus' combination is disgusting and vivid. Combined with Jeff apparently escalating to physical assault and Jonah's 'Ow!' the whole sequence is a perfect small sitcom scene.

jokes.text: You cockless cockroach! / You pile of failure shaped like a rapist! / I am not shaped like a rapist!
jokes.setup: The Jeff-Jonah fight is escalating.
jokes.punchline: 'Pile of failure shaped like a rapist' — Jonah's indignant objection ('I am not shaped like a rapist!') is the funniest response possible to an insult.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best joke-response pairs. 'Pile of failure shaped like a rapist' is an outstanding insult — 'shaped like' as a descriptor is brilliantly odd (it's a shape accusation, not a behavior accusation). Jonah's 'I am not shaped like a rapist!' as his chosen point of objection is perfect — he's not denying 'pile of failure,' just the physical comparison — which implies he's accepted the first half. Highly quotable, strong structure.

jokes.text: I'm gonna hit you with my car, like I did in kindergarten!
jokes.setup: The fight between Jeff and Jonah continues to escalate.
jokes.punchline: Jeff threatening to repeat a childhood car assault — the revelation that he actually hit Jonah with a car in kindergarten.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy. The escalation from 'I'll hit you with my car' to 'like I did in kindergarten' — the past-tense precedent — is the crucial element. This implies Jeff has established a pattern of running over Jonah with cars, has been doing it since they were children, and considers it a legitimate conflict resolution tool. The matter-of-fact historical reference is perfect character comedy for Jeff.

jokes.text: Okay, fine! Jesus fuckin' Christ, I'll be vice president, just stop yelling at me! / All right, fine. / Crisis is now averted. / No. I have one condition.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just been beaten into accepting the VP slot.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's 'Crisis is now averted' — delivered as the all-clear for Jonah accepting the second-most powerful job in the world through sustained verbal and physical abuse — is the button. Then Jonah immediately introduces a condition.
jokes.explanation: Solid scene button — 'Crisis is now averted' treating the coerced acceptance of the VP slot as a crisis resolution is good understatement. The immediate 'I have one condition' pivot keeps the scene moving and Jonah's stubbornness consistent.

jokes.text: I want Richard to be Secretary of farm shit. / We're gonna have to fire Dan. / Why do you want to fire him? / Uh, just as a way of saying sorry for yelling at you earlier. / You know, feminism. / That sounds great. Fire Dan. He sucks.
jokes.setup: Jonah is naming his conditions for accepting the VP slot.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'You know, feminism' as a one-word justification for firing Dan is absurd, and Jonah's enthusiastic 'That sounds great. Fire Dan. He sucks.' — agreeing without understanding.
jokes.explanation: Multiple good beats: 'Secretary of farm shit' as Richard's cabinet title, Gary's inexplicable 'feminism' justification, and Jonah immediately approving the firing of Dan (his own condition for the discussion). The randomness of 'feminism' as a reason is perfectly Veep.

jokes.text: Catherine, it is just the party platform. It's like a to-do list of things we're not gonna do. / 'Restore faith in democracy?' / I mean, we couldn't do that even if we wanted to.
jokes.setup: Catherine is confronting Selina about trading away same-sex marriage.
jokes.punchline: 'A to-do list of things we're not gonna do' is the joke — a perfect definition of a party platform. 'We couldn't do that even if we wanted to' about restoring faith in democracy is the button.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best satire lines. 'A to-do list of things we're not gonna do' is an elegant, quotable, and genuinely insightful definition of a party platform — it could be a real quote from a political operative. The 'Restore faith in democracy' example is perfectly chosen, and 'We couldn't do that even if we wanted to' is devastating. This is the episode's sharpest political satire.

jokes.text: Casino licenses in Macao. / Oh, and... Did you hear that, Keith? / I did. / How could you? / Okay, Macao.
jokes.setup: Selina has just won the nomination. Sherman Tanz immediately pivots to transactional interests.
jokes.punchline: The immediate corruption pivot — 'Casino licenses in Macao' — as the first post-nomination conversation item. Selina's theatrical shock ('Did you hear that, Keith? How could you?') before immediately saying 'Okay, Macao.'
jokes.explanation: The speed of the pivot — nomination to quid pro quo in seconds — is the joke. Selina's performed outrage ('How could you?') followed instantly by agreement ('Okay, Macao') is perfect character-defining comedy: she's not even a little surprised, just performs the outrage for the room.

jokes.text: Catherine, it is just the party platform. It's like a to-do list of things we're not gonna do. 'Restore faith in democracy?' I mean, we couldn't do that even if we wanted to.
jokes.setup: Catherine confronts Selina about trading away same-sex marriage.
jokes.punchline: The party platform as 'a to-do list of things we're not gonna do' — a cynical but accurate framing. The example of 'Restore faith in democracy' as something beyond their capability is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest political satire lines. 'A to-do list of things we're not gonna do' is genuinely insightful political commentary delivered as a throwaway joke. 'Restore faith in democracy? We couldn't do that even if we wanted to' is a devastating and quotable summary of American politics. Both funny and genuinely cutting.

jokes.text: Oh, well, if I had a dollar for every 'Mother, I will never forgive you...' / Bazillionaire. / Correct.
jokes.setup: Catherine has just said she will never forgive Selina for trading away same-sex marriage.
jokes.punchline: Gary answers Selina's rhetorical setup ('I'd have a dollar for every...') with 'Bazillionaire' — confirming the frequency — and Selina's flat 'Correct' is the button.
jokes.explanation: 'Bazillionaire' as a specifically quantified hyperbole is good, and Selina's 'Correct' is a strong deadpan confirmation. The joke works because Gary is answering a rhetorical question with a number — he takes the hypothetical literally, which is a Gary character beat.

jokes.text: Why is Jonah helping you? / Started from the bottom, now we here.
jokes.setup: Marjorie asks Jonah why he's suddenly cooperating with Selina.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's Drake reference — 'Started from the bottom, now we here' — applied to his trajectory from hostile rival to coerced VP.
jokes.explanation: A modest pop culture reference that works mainly because of the irony — the 'bottom' for Jonah was being berated and physically threatened into accepting second-most-powerful-job-in-the-world. The gap between Drake's meaning and Jonah's reality is the joke.

jokes.text: Are you out of your fucking mind, Gram-Ma'am? / No. Oh, my God. Okay. / I will never forgive you for this! / Mmm? Yep. / Ka-ching.
jokes.setup: Catherine's son/Marjorie's son has just learned about Selina's deal on same-sex marriage.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'Ka-ching' — treating each 'I will never forgive you' as money depositing into the bazillionaire account — is the callback payoff.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. Gary's 'Ka-ching' — triggered by the second 'I will never forgive you' in the same scene — completes the running gag established seconds earlier. The joke is in Gary immediately implementing the hypothetical financial tracking system, which is perfectly Gary. One of the episode's most satisfying structural payoffs.

jokes.text: That DeVito's a hell of a speechwriter.
jokes.setup: Selina is delivering her nomination acceptance speech, which is composed largely of lines stolen from DeVito's nominating speech.
jokes.punchline: Selina commenting on her own speech — written by DeVito — from the stage.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback payoff to the speech-theft subplot. Selina complimenting the speechwriter whose words she's reading — without realizing or caring about the irony — is good character comedy and a satisfying payoff to the episode's running joke.

jokes.text: It'd be faster just to lay down and roll.
jokes.setup: DeVito is making his slow way to the stage to give his nominating speech.
jokes.punchline: The suggestion that rolling would be faster than DeVito's mobility is delivered deadpan — casual cruelty about his war injuries.
jokes.explanation: A quick, callous one-liner about DeVito's disability. Functional dark comedy but not elevated beyond the obvious.

jokes.text: Anyway, listen, it's kind of important, actually, and I would also say... / Okay, yeah. / That it's not easy... / Okay. / And it's kinda also not fair. / You want coffee from across the street? Yeah? / Ah! I got it. Little chia seed. / Nah. Never mind. Forget I ever said anything. / You look beautiful. / And you are a lifesaver.
jokes.setup: Selina is attempting to fire Gary before her nomination acceptance speech.
jokes.punchline: Selina's repeated failed attempts to fire Gary are deflected by his obliviousness — he offers coffee, finds a chia seed in her teeth, and she abandons the attempt entirely with 'Forget I ever said anything.'
jokes.explanation: The best sustained cringe beat in the episode. The comedy is entirely in Gary's perfect obliviousness — his instinct to manage Selina's physical needs (coffee, chia seed) is so powerful it prevents her from firing him. 'Forget I ever said anything' / 'You look beautiful' / 'And you are a lifesaver' — Selina capitulating not because she forgave him but because Gary found a chia seed — is devastating and perfect character comedy. The structure builds exquisitely through each failed attempt. Character integration is a perfect 10 for both.

jokes.text: Good evening, Mr. Cafferty! You ready for your sponge bath?
jokes.setup: Cut to Ben in the hospital at the exact moment Selina's acceptance speech is playing.
jokes.punchline: Ben's hospital nurse calling him 'Mr. Cafferty' — Ben doesn't even get his real name at the moment of his career's culmination.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition is the joke — Ben, who has worked his whole life for this moment, is alone in a hospital being called by the wrong name and offered a sponge bath while Selina takes her bow. The cut itself is the punchline.

jokes.text: Ah, call me Ben.
jokes.setup: The nurse has just called Ben 'Mr. Cafferty.'
jokes.punchline: Ben, in the midst of his apparent final hospitalization, corrects his name — 'call me Ben' — with the same weary pragmatism he applies to everything.
jokes.explanation: Three words, but they land with Ben's characteristic flat pragmatism. The whole scene is sad-funny — Ben on his potential deathbed, having his correct name politely established with a nurse. Character integration is very high.

jokes.text: President Meyer offered me Secretary of Agriculture. / Yes, Richard! Secretary of Swag-riculture! Oh, my God. Yes! I'm goin' back to Washington, baby!
jokes.setup: Richard reveals his cabinet appointment.
jokes.punchline: 'Secretary of Swag-riculture' — Dan's enthusiastic rebrand of the Agriculture Department.
jokes.explanation: 'Swag-riculture' is a decent pun, Dan's excitement about going back to Washington is character-consistent. A functional laugh, nothing exceptional.

jokes.text: But the 'but' was, 'But you have to fire Dan.' / So, I guess you're fired. / What? / Thanks for making this easy on me. / I gotta get out of politics. / To be honest, I never thought you were really cut out for it, anyway.
jokes.setup: Richard is telling Dan he has good news and no 'buts.'
jokes.punchline: Richard fires Dan with 'So, I guess you're fired' and then 'Thanks for making this easy on me' — and his final 'To be honest, I never thought you were really cut out for it, anyway' is an honest, unmalicious gut-punch.
jokes.explanation: Richard's farewell to Dan is one of the episode's finest character moments. 'I never thought you were really cut out for it, anyway' — delivered without cruelty, as genuine honest assessment — is the funniest and most devastating thing Richard could say. It's not an insult; it's Richard being kind by finally being honest. The 'Thanks for making this easy on me' is also perfect Richard — he thanks people for accommodating his discomfort, even when he's the one doing the difficult thing.

jokes.text: To be honest, I never thought you were really cut out for it, anyway.
jokes.setup: Dan says he has to get out of politics after being fired.
jokes.punchline: Richard's candid, completely sincere agreement — 'I never thought you were cut out for it' — delivered without malice, making it both an insult and a kind of pastoral wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Richard's total sincerity in this devastation makes it perfectly Richard. He's not trying to wound Dan — he genuinely thinks he's offering useful perspective. The absence of cruelty in the cruelest assessment is the joke.

jokes.text: Congratulations to you, Amy! / Thank you! / You're gonna be Jonah's chief of staff. / Huh? / I don't like the way you talked to me the other day. / Uh-huh. / Yeah. Yeah. / Yes!
jokes.setup: Selina congratulates Amy at the victory celebration.
jokes.punchline: The 'congratulations' is actually an assignment to be Jonah Ryan's chief of staff — a demotion and punishment. Amy's processing through denial, resigned 'uh-huh,' and then her private 'Yes!' suggests she's resolved to accept it (or celebrate something else).
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy — Selina weaponizing congratulations as punishment. Amy's 'Yes!' at the end is the best beat: ambiguous whether she's accepting defeat with grace or somehow finding a positive.

jokes.text: He's a super Veep / Super Veep, he's super Veepy / Yow!
jokes.setup: Jonah is celebrating his VP nomination.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's spontaneous, original self-hype song — 'Super Veep, he's super Veepy' — delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: 'Super Veepy' is inspired — it takes the title 'Vice President' and renders it in the vocabulary of a children's TV character while Jonah performs it with athletic-celebration energy. Pure Jonah absurdism.

jokes.text: Hi, Sue. / Sue, the Vice President would like a minute with the President. / The President told me to pencil you in to half past go-fuck-yourself.
jokes.setup: Jonah, now VP, tries to use his title to gain access to Selina.
jokes.punchline: 'Half past go-fuck-yourself' — Sue's time-based dismissal, combining schedule management with profanity.
jokes.explanation: 'Half past go-fuck-yourself' is the best pure line of the episode — it combines Sue's professional vocabulary (scheduling, penciling in) with maximum hostility, delivered with zero affect. The construction is elegant: it sounds like a real time ('half past four') but is an insult. Highly quotable, perfect economy, perfect character integration. Sue's subsequent exchanges ('If you want to work for me...' 'I don't') are also excellent Sue character beats.

jokes.text: Sue, when I am President... / You won't be. / If you want to work for me... / I don't. / Then I would start treating me with the respect that I deserve. / I am. / Sue, as Vice President of the United States, I order you to let me into the Oval Office! / God damn it!
jokes.setup: Jonah continues trying to assert authority over Sue.
jokes.punchline: Sue's flat two-word denials ('You won't be,' 'I don't,' 'I am') to each of Jonah's threats are perfect, with the final absurdist escalation to 'I ORDER you' and Jonah's self-directed 'God damn it!'
jokes.explanation: Sue's three consecutive two-word denials are structurally perfect — each of Jonah's threats is turned back on him with minimum effort ('I am' responding to 'I deserve respect' is devastating). The escalation to a presidential ORDER followed by Jonah cursing at himself is a great payoff. Sue-Jonah is arguably the best micro-dynamic in the episode.

jokes.text: Sue, when I am President... / You won't be. / If you want to work for me... / I don't. / Then I would start treating me with the respect that I deserve. / I am. / Sue, as Vice President of the United States, I order you to let me into the Oval Office! / God damn it!
jokes.setup: Jonah tries various tactics to get past Sue.
jokes.punchline: Every gambit Jonah uses — future authority, employment threat, dignity claim, direct order — is demolished by Sue in one sentence each. Her 'I am' to 'treating me with the respect I deserve' is the peak. Jonah breaking on 'God damn it!' is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The Sue-demolishes-Jonah escalation is a masterpiece of comedic structure. Each exchange is a setup-punchline in itself — Jonah escalates the threat level, Sue responds with the minimum words necessary to destroy it. 'I am' as the response to 'treat me with the respect I deserve' is the comedic peak: she IS treating him with the respect he deserves (none). The escalation to 'I order you!' and his own frustrated expletive is the perfect collapse. This is sitcom scene construction at an elite level.

jokes.text: Ma'am, Joint Chiefs are getting a little squirrely about Chinese tank movements near the Tibetan border. / Oh, my God. What a bunch of pussies.
jokes.setup: Selina is just taking the Oval Office, receiving her first briefings as nominee.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to a potential Chinese military provocation near Tibet is 'What a bunch of pussies' — referring to the Joint Chiefs' concern, not the Chinese.
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Selina's response to Joint Chiefs' military concerns is to dismiss them as weak. The joke previews the flash-forward payoff (Tibet is briefly freed, then presumably lost). Character-specific, economical.

jokes.text: Ma'am, we're expecting a call from the Israeli prime minister about the Palestinian food riots. / That reminds me, I'm starving. Gary! / Oh, ma'am, Gary doesn't work here.
jokes.setup: Aide mentions Palestinian food riots.
jokes.punchline: Selina's free-association from 'food riots' to 'I'm starving' — and her instinct to call Gary despite firing him.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. The free-association from 'Palestinian food riots' to 'I'm starving' shows Selina's foreign policy as self-referential. Then calling Gary — who she just (almost) fired — reveals that even in the Oval Office, her instinct is to call him. The aide's matter-of-fact 'Gary doesn't work here' and Selina's response ('I know. Why are you telling me things that I already know?') is the best button.
