jokes.text: I know. Why are you telling me things that I already know? / Sorry. Yes. / You know? / But I would be more than happy to get you something to eat. What would you like? / You figure that out. / Okay. / Why am I coming up with solutions for you? That's your job, right? That's not my job.
jokes.setup: Selina is replacing Gary's function with a new aide.
jokes.punchline: Selina demands the aide figure out food, then immediately asks why the aide is making HER come up with solutions — a perfectly circular management style where delegating is also the aide's fault.
jokes.explanation: The perfect Selina management style crystallized: she delegates, then blames the delegate for making her delegate. 'Why am I coming up with solutions for you? That's your job, right?' — delivered after she told the aide to 'figure it out' — is pure Selina circular logic. This is Gary's replacement failing to be Gary.

jokes.text: David? Shalom. / So, what did the Palestinians do this time? / David, I have to tell you, my daughter Catherine was exactly the same way, a whiner.
jokes.setup: Selina takes a call from the Israeli prime minister about Palestinian food riots.
jokes.punchline: She compares Palestinian food riot grievances to Catherine's childhood whining — equating geopolitical humanitarian crisis with a teenager's complaints.
jokes.explanation: The comparison of Palestinian grievances to Catherine's whining is Selina's solipsism reaching its logical endpoint — everything reduces to her personal experience. 'Shalom' as an opener is a nice casual appropriation. 'What did the Palestinians do this time?' as a diplomatic opening is perfectly awful. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Jesus Christ. / No, no. Not you, David.
jokes.setup: Selina is on the phone with the Israeli prime minister when her glasses arrive and she reacts audibly.
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately clarifying to the Israeli PM that 'Jesus Christ' wasn't directed at him — a perfect diplomatic fumble.
jokes.explanation: Perfect micro-gag. 'Jesus Christ' as an expletive directed at the Israeli PM — and Selina's instant damage control — is sharp. The specificity of 'Not you, David' implies she knows exactly how badly that landed. Economy is nearly flawless.

jokes.text: The flash-forward cold open — Mike announcing Selina's funeral, noting she 'permanently overturned same-sex marriage' and her age was '76 or possibly 77 or, according to some sources, 75.'
jokes.setup: A flash-forward reveals the end of Selina's story — her funeral, covered by Mike on TV.
jokes.punchline: Her legacy includes permanently overturning same-sex marriage, and even her age is uncertain — three different figures given.
jokes.explanation: The flash-forward payoff to the entire episode's arc — Selina got the nomination, won the presidency, and her most lasting legacy is overturning same-sex marriage (the deal she made with Buddy). The uncertainty about her age (three different figures) is a perfect detail — her vanity about her age follows her to her obituary. Structurally, the entire episode exists to set up this reveal.

jokes.text: ...Meyer is fondly remembered for briefly freeing what was once known as the nation of Tibet, as well as for permanently overturning same-sex marriage.
jokes.setup: Mike lists Selina Meyer's presidential legacy.
jokes.punchline: 'Briefly freeing Tibet' (a failed policy) vs. 'permanently overturning same-sex marriage' (a casual political deal) as her two defining legacies — the former being accidental and temporary, the latter being a petty transaction that lasted forever.
jokes.explanation: The perfect payoff for the entire episode's political cynicism arc. 'Briefly freeing Tibet' and 'permanently overturning same-sex marriage' as Selina Meyer's presidential legacy is one of the most devastating comedy punchlines in prestige TV satire. The contrast — the good thing was brief and accidental, the bad thing (done for naked political advantage) was permanent — is a complete statement about the show's themes delivered in eleven words. 'What was once known as the nation of Tibet' implies she also destroyed Tibetan statehood in the process. Exceptional craft.

jokes.text: Among the dignitaries personally selected by Selina Meyer to sit in the front row are many former rivals in a Meyer-esque gesture of grace and goodwill.
jokes.setup: Mike describes the funeral seating.
jokes.punchline: 'Meyer-esque gesture of grace and goodwill' — framing something Selina did as graceful when we know she chose who sits in the front row as a final act of petty control and point-scoring.
jokes.explanation: 'Meyer-esque gesture of grace and goodwill' as a spin on what is obviously her last manipulation is nicely ironic. The compound of 'grace and goodwill' applied to Selina strains credibility even from Mike.

jokes.text: How does Mike have more hair than I do? / Maybe because he has two testicles.
jokes.setup: Characters are watching Mike deliver Selina's funeral coverage.
jokes.punchline: The response to a hair-envy question is a testicle-count comparison.
jokes.explanation: Quick dark joke — the implicit suggestion that one character has fewer than two testicles (possibly a callback to Jonah's condition, or just a general insult). The non-sequitur logic is amusing.

jokes.text: Oh, Montez looks good. I'd still do her. / Count me in.
jokes.setup: Former President Montez is attending Selina's funeral.
jokes.punchline: Characters at the funeral engaging in inappropriate attraction commentary about the former president.
jokes.explanation: A modest gag. The inappropriateness of the setting (funeral) makes the attraction comment slightly darker, but it doesn't build to anything specific. 'Count me in' is a good quick response.

jokes.text: I didn't want to go to that stupid funeral, anyway. / Well, then it's a good thing that you got impeached, they didn't have to invite you.
jokes.setup: Jonah is at home watching the funeral instead of attending.
jokes.punchline: Beth's casual explanation — 'you got impeached' as the logistical reason he wasn't invited — reveals both that Jonah was impeached and that Beth is completely unbothered by this.
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Jonah was impeached is delivered as a throwaway domestic correction — Beth's casualness about the constitutional crisis is perfect. 'It's a good thing you got impeached' as a silver lining is beautifully dark. The reveal of Jonah's presidency-ending scandal as a funeral logistic is structurally excellent.

jokes.text: I didn't want to go to that stupid funeral, anyway. / Well, then it's a good thing that you got impeached, they didn't have to invite you.
jokes.setup: Someone is rationalizing not being invited to Selina's funeral.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that they were impeached — explaining their exclusion — delivered as a consolation.
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy — the revelation that one of these characters was impeached is delivered as casual small talk. The payoff works as a quick worldbuilding gag about the characters' futures.

jokes.text: You know, Richard, there are few things in life more difficult than the loss of a parent. / Who wants margaritas?
jokes.setup: Dan offers condolences to Richard about Selina's death.
jokes.punchline: Richard's immediate 'Who wants margaritas?' — pivoting from Dan's attempted poignancy to celebration — reveals Richard's relationship to death as uncomplicated and his practical hospitality instinct.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'few things more difficult than loss of a parent' to 'who wants margaritas?' is perfectly timed misdirection. Richard isn't being callous — he genuinely thinks refreshments are appropriate. His warmth and his obliviousness are the same quality. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: The Presidential motorcade has arrived, and here comes President of the United States Richard Splett and the First Lady, Annette Splett, somber but elegant, basking in the glow of a landslide re-election following President Splett's historic three-state solution Middle East peace agreement, for which he won the Nobel Prize.
jokes.setup: The flash-forward shows who eventually became president.
jokes.punchline: Richard Splett — the most guileless, seemingly incompetent character — won the presidency, was re-elected in a landslide, solved Middle East peace, and won the Nobel Prize.
jokes.explanation: The show's final and most satisfying satirical joke: the only person in the entire run of Veep who is genuinely decent, without ego or malice, turns out to be the one who actually accomplishes great things. Richard's presidency is the series' thesis statement — the system is so broken that only someone without ambition could actually serve it well. The three-state solution Nobel Prize is the perfect specific detail. Structurally, this is the payoff to the entire episode (and season). The contrast with Selina's legacy (overturning same-sex marriage) makes it devastating.

jokes.text: We have 40 head of alpaca now, 17 on the way. My daughter's stepping down from NASA to take over the ranch, so, I can focus on my watch-making. / We didn't have kids. Best decision we ever made. Best. Best. / Our greyhounds are our kids.
jokes.setup: The flash-forward shows various characters' futures.
jokes.punchline: Richard's idyllic ranch life (alpacas, watchmaking, NASA daughter); an unnamed couple's blissful childlessness; greyhounds as children.
jokes.explanation: Richard's retirement details are funny in their specificity (watchmaking, alpacas) — the kind of pastoral happiness that is genuinely incongruous for a former President of the United States. 'Best. Best. Best.' (childlessness) is a good deadpan repeat.

jokes.text: We didn't have kids. Best decision we ever made. / Best. Best. / Our greyhounds are our kids.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy have apparently ended up together and childless.
jokes.punchline: Their synchronized 'Best. Best.' and the follow-up 'Our greyhounds are our kids' — both perfectly capturing the specific flavor of DINK smugness — and the detail of greyhounds as the animal choice.
jokes.explanation: Dan and Amy together, childless, smug, with greyhounds is a perfectly chosen fate for the two most toxic political operatives. Greyhounds specifically — fast, elegant, neurotic, high-maintenance — mirror their personalities exactly. The synchronized 'Best' is the comedy beat.

jokes.text: Kent, do you still keep in touch with Ben's widow? / I forget her name. / No. / Betcha Ben did, too.
jokes.setup: The funeral attendees are reminiscing about Ben.
jokes.punchline: 'Betcha Ben did, too' — the implication that Ben didn't know his own wife's name — is the callback payoff to Ben's earlier 'Don't tell my wife and kids' / 'I've never met them' exchange.
jokes.explanation: An excellent late-episode callback to the Ben hospital scene. 'I forget her name' / 'Betcha Ben did, too' — the joke that Ben's own wife was a stranger to everyone, including possibly Ben himself — is a perfect dark character payoff. Economy is excellent and the callback structure earns the laugh.

jokes.text: Amazing he hung on as long as he did, the fat fuck. / Excuse me. / Sorry.
jokes.setup: Characters are remembering Ben at his funeral.
jokes.punchline: A warm tribute to Ben's longevity immediately punctuated by 'the fat fuck' — and the instant social correction/retraction.
jokes.explanation: A quick dark joke — the tender tribute immediately undercut by 'the fat fuck' is the standard Veep tonal move. The immediate social correction ('Excuse me' / 'Sorry') makes it funnier. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Amazing he hung on as long as he did, the fat fuck. / Excuse me. / Sorry.
jokes.setup: Someone is offering a Ben eulogy at the reception.
jokes.punchline: 'The fat fuck' as an affectionate obituary term, followed by the immediate social correction and apology — the profane admiration for Ben's survival is perfectly calibrated dark comedy.
jokes.explanation: The profane affection of 'the fat fuck' as an obituary compliment, immediately corrected by social convention, is a quick dark beat. Works as a punctuation joke in the funeral sequence.

jokes.text: Dan. / I think the last time I saw you, your wife was being born. / 2019, yeah.
jokes.setup: Amy spots Dan at the funeral after many years.
jokes.punchline: Amy's insult — 'the last time I saw you, your wife was being born' — implies Dan married someone extremely young. Dan's flat confirmation of the year ('2019, yeah') is the perfect response.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The insult ('your wife was being born') is devastating — implying Dan married someone so young she was literally an infant the last time they met. But the funniest element is Dan's flat acceptance: 'Yeah, 2019.' He doesn't object to the premise, just confirms the year. This tells you everything about Dan — his moral self-awareness is zero, and he treats his predatory marriage as simply a data point. Perfect structure, economy, and character integration.

jokes.text: How long's it been, Ames? / Dan. / I think the last time I saw you, your wife was being born. / 2019, yeah.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are reuniting at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: 'The last time I saw you, your wife was being born' — implying Dan's wife is extraordinarily young — and Amy's casual confirmation '2019, yeah' makes it a real date.
jokes.explanation: Dan's extreme age gap marriage played out as a scheduling joke — 'when I last saw you, your wife was being born.' Amy's confirmation with a year ('2019, yeah') as if this is not remarkable is the perfect deadpan. The joke reveals Dan's predictable sleaziness has followed him into old age.

jokes.text: Look at you losers waitin' around to make sure she's actually dead, while you sit in the back row suckin' on hard candies like what, Will?
jokes.setup: Jeff is addressing the gathering at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: Setting up the joke about Will eating hard candies, then turning it over to Will for the punchline.
jokes.explanation: The setup for the Will punchline. Jeff's funeral-crashing energy is consistent with his character. 'Waitin' around to make sure she's actually dead' is a good dark line.

jokes.text: I like jizz. / Ah, shit. He ain't what he used to be.
jokes.setup: Jeff asks Will what flavor hard candy he's eating.
jokes.punchline: Will's answer — 'I like jizz' — is a complete non-sequitur response to a candy question (possibly dementia, possibly Will being Will). Jeff's 'He ain't what he used to be' is the perfect fond eulogy for Will's cognitive decline.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Will's earlier sexual masochism speech. In the flash-forward, Will appears to have declined or simply answered a candy question with 'I like jizz' — which is both a non-sequitur and possibly consistent with his established character. 'He ain't what he used to be' as Jeff's eulogy for Will's mind is a great deadpan line. The callback structure is elegant.

jokes.text: You'd hate the flowers. / But I... / I brought the Dubonnet.
jokes.setup: Gary is at Selina's grave, clearly distraught, talking to her casket.
jokes.punchline: Gary bringing Selina's discontinued favorite lipstick color to her funeral — the one she was saving 'for when I get the nomination' — is the emotional payoff of the episode, but the joke is in Gary still managing her preferences after death.
jokes.explanation: The most emotionally complex joke in the episode — possibly in the series. Gary bringing Selina her discontinued Dubonnet at her funeral, because she saved it for the nomination she finally got, is devastating. The comedy is in Gary's absolute consistency — he is still managing her needs after her death. But the tragedy is that she saved it for this moment and never got to wear it. 'You'd hate the flowers' establishes the premise perfectly. This is genuinely exceptional writing that operates as comedy and tragedy simultaneously. The character integration is a perfect 10.

jokes.text: And it looks like the soldiers are having a little trouble. They're fumbling around the crypt, but they cannot seem to get it to respond in any way. / Typical. / But now, they've got it. And there she goes, sliding right in there.
jokes.setup: The pallbearers are struggling to place Selina's casket in the crypt during the live TV broadcast.
jokes.punchline: Mike's increasingly suggestive funeral commentary ('cannot seem to get it to respond in any way,' 'sliding right in there'), punctuated by someone's 'Typical.'
jokes.explanation: Mike's accidental double entendres during the funeral broadcast are good Mike character comedy — he's always been oblivious to subtext. 'Typical' as the response to 'cannot get it to respond in any way' is a strong beat. The commentary on Selina's sexual legacy (implied) is dark.

jokes.text: And it looks like the soldiers are having a little trouble. They're fumbling around the crypt, but they cannot seem to get it to respond in any way. / Typical.
jokes.setup: Soldiers are trying to slide Selina's casket into the crypt.
jokes.punchline: 'Typical' — the single-word verdict on Selina Meyer's resistant crypt entry, applying her personal stubbornness to her physical remains.
jokes.explanation: One perfect word. The soldiers' difficulty getting Selina into the crypt — she won't even die cooperatively — answered by 'Typical' is an outstanding single-word joke. It functions as a final character assessment: she was difficult in life and difficult in death. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: But first, as someone who served with President Meyer for over two decades, I feel I'd be remiss if I did not offer my own heartfelt eulogy to a president who many feel was very underrated and deserved... / I'm sorry. Breaking news. I've just been told that four-time Academy Award-winning actor Tom Hanks has died at the age of 88...
jokes.setup: Mike is about to deliver a personal eulogy for Selina, the president he served for decades.
jokes.punchline: Mike interrupts his own eulogy for Tom Hanks's death — immediately abandoning Selina mid-sentence for a more culturally significant figure.
jokes.explanation: The series finale's ultimate joke and one of the finest in the show's run. The entire final act — the funeral, the eulogies, the dignitaries — is undercut by Mike immediately pivoting to Tom Hanks dying. But the brilliance is in the accumulation: (1) Mike being the one to deliver Selina's eulogy (the man she held in contempt), (2) him breaking away mid-eulogy, (3) Tom Hanks as the specific replacement (beloved, genuinely great, everything Selina was not), (4) the extended Tom Hanks tribute ('American everyman... comedy and drama... towering and beloved figure') serving as the real eulogy Selina never got. The show's thesis — Selina's life was small and consequential only in damage — is delivered through this act of journalistic abandonment. Nearly perfect comedy writing.

tmdb_overview: The nominating fight between Selina and her rivals reaches its climax, as their race comes to a historic finish. Series finale.
insight: Series finale delivers 90 jokes in 90 minutes but lands below elite threshold.
